figures.gloss: The absolute, formless reality from which the universe emanates. Lingayat theology holds that this Supreme Being modifies itself into both the material world and the individual soul, rendering the universe completely real, not a Maya (illusion).
figures.facet: Unlike mainstream Shaivism where Shiva is worshipped in monumental temples through Brahmin priests, Parasiva is utterly formless and uncontainable in static architecture, accessible solely through the microcosm of the personal Ishtalinga.

figures.gloss: An ovoid, slate-colored emblem of Parasiva, given at birth and worn constantly on the body. It serves as a personal, physical anchor for the divine, visualizing the wearer's soul and the cosmos as a single entity.
figures.facet: Utterly obliviates the need for temples, pilgrimages, and priestly mediation. Divinity is radically democratized; everyone, regardless of gender or former caste, carries the absolute directly on their chest.

figures.gloss: The 12th-century philosopher, poet, and statesman who ignited the Veerashaiva movement. He envisioned an egalitarian society rooted in radical bhakti (devotion), dismantling the oppressive orthodoxies of temple-worship, caste segregation, and gender discrimination.
figures.facet: He didn't just preach theology; he built a radical, parallel society (the Anubhava Mantapa) that actively intermarried untouchables with Brahmins, triggering a violent political revolution that threatened the foundations of the medieval social order.

figures.gloss: A radical 12th-century female mystic who rejected an earthly marriage to a king, betrothed herself completely to 'Channa Mallikarjuna' (Shiva), and wandered naked, covered only by her long hair, to join the Anubhava Mantapa.
figures.facet: Stripping away all societal constructs of female modesty, she demanded intellectual equality in the assembly of saints, viewing all worldly men as illusions and exposing the profound irrelevance of the physical body.

figures.gloss: The supreme patron saint of the Veerashaiva movement and president of the Anubhava Mantapa. A master of the 'Bedagina Vachanas' (riddling poems), he served as the ultimate spiritual touchstone for all other mystics.
figures.facet: Wields paradox, irony, and negation to ruthlessly shatter the ego. He constantly challenged even Basavanna and Akka Mahadevi, exposing the slightest traces of spiritual pride or lingering attachments to physical devotion.

figures.gloss: The wandering mendicant or realized soul, considered the 'moving Linga'. In Veerashaiva doctrine, feeding or revering a Jangama is not just an act of charity, but identical to worshipping Shiva directly.
figures.facet: Elevates the living, breathing human being above any stone idol. Basavanna declared that the static temple ('sthavara') perishes, but the moving, breathing temple ('jangama') stays forever, placing human life at the cosmic center.

figures.gloss: 'That which is said'—rhythmic, free-verse poetry composed in vernacular Kannada. It bypassed elite Sanskrit to deliver direct, unmediated spiritual experience and fierce social critique directly to the common people.
figures.facet: Rejects scriptural authority and elite linguistic gatekeeping. The poetry is anti-clerical, drawing profound metaphysical metaphors from everyday labor—tailoring, washing, smithing—proving that divine truth speaks in the dialect of the streets.

figures.gloss: The radical doctrine that 'Work is Heaven' ('Kayakave Kailasa'). It insists that every occupation, no matter how 'polluting' in the traditional caste system, is a direct, fully realized path to spiritual liberation.
figures.facet: Directly attacks the Hindu concept of Karma and ritual purity. A cobbler's work is as spiritually potent as a priest's chanting, completely abolishing the spiritual hierarchy of labor and demanding strict economic self-reliance.
figures.provenance: Vachanas of Nuliya Chandayya and Aydakki Marayya.

figures.gloss: Basava's nephew and a leading vachana poet in his own right, credited with systematizing the shatsthala scheme into a formal doctrinal structure and leading the sharanas' exodus after the persecution that scattered the Kalyana movement (c. 1167-68).
figures.facet: Where Basava and Allama supplied the movement's charisma and paradox, Channabasavanna supplied its architecture — turning a body of devotional poetry into a transmissible theological system that could survive its founders' deaths and a violent dispersal.
figures.provenance: Vachanas of Channabasavanna; Basava Purana (Palkuriki Somanatha).

figures.gloss: The 'hall of spiritual experience' established at Kalyana, where sharanas of every caste and both sexes gathered to debate theology as equals, presided over by Allama Prabhu seated on the Shunya Simhasana (throne of the void).
figures.facet: A parliament of mystics rather than a monastery: its authority ran on open, cross-caste, mixed-gender debate rather than lineage or ordination, making the institution itself the movement's most radical claim.

figures.gloss: The six-stage path of the devotee's ascent — bhakta, mahesha, prasadi, pranalingi, sharana, and aikya — by which the individual soul (anga) is progressively refined until it achieves complete, indistinguishable union with the linga (divine principle).
figures.facet: Salvation modeled as a graded refinement rather than a single leap of grace or a sudden enlightenment: each stage names a specific, checkable change in the devotee's relationship to the divine, work Channabasavanna's writings formalize.

figures.gloss: The eight 'coverings' or shields that structure a practicing Lingayat's daily life: guru, linga (the ishtalinga), jangama, padodaka (water that has washed the guru's feet), prasada (sanctified food), vibhuti (sacred ash), rudraksha beads, and the shadakshara mantra.
figures.facet: Discipline distributed across eight ordinary, repeatable practices rather than concentrated into rare high rites — sanctity is scheduled into daily life instead of reserved for festival occasions.

figures.gloss: A weaver-saint of Mudanuru active in the 10th-11th century, generations before Basava, who addressed Shiva as 'Ramanatha' in terse, image-driven vachanas — evidence that the vernacular devotional-poetry stream predates the Kalyana reform movement it is often collapsed into.
figures.facet: His priority complicates the standard founder-narrative: the vachana form and its plainspoken anti-ritual voice already existed as a working idiom a century before Basava turned it into an organized social revolution.
figures.provenance: Vachanas of Devara Dasimayya, anthologized with Basavanna, Akka Mahadevi, and Allama Prabhu in A. K. Ramanujan's 'Speaking of Siva' (Penguin Classics, 1973).

figures.gloss: A 12th-century sharana of Sonnalige (Sholapur) counted among the movement's great vachana authors, known for building irrigation tanks and fusing public works with devotion before being folded, in tradition, into the Anubhava Mantapa circle.
figures.facet: He gives the kayaka doctrine a concrete civic form: 'work is worship' realized not as metaphor but as literal tank-building infrastructure serving the surrounding community.

figures.gloss: The movement's principal hagiography, composed in Telugu by Palkuriki Somanatha in the 13th century and later rendered into Kannada, narrating Basava's life and the deeds of the Kalyana sharanas.
figures.facet: The founder's life recorded not by a disinterested chronicler but by a devotee writing scripture-as-biography, fusing hagiography and history into the same act of transmission.
figures.provenance: Palkuriki Somanatha, Basava Purana (Telugu, 13th c.); later Kannada recensions.

figures.gloss: The confluence of the Krishna and Malaprabha rivers whose resident lord, Kudalasangamadeva, is Basava's chosen personal form of Shiva and the signature (ankita) under which nearly every one of his vachanas is composed; the site holds his samadhi and remains a living pilgrimage center.
figures.facet: A god fused inseparably to one river-junction — Basava's universal reformist theology is nonetheless signed, vachana after vachana, with the name of a single, specific place.

figures.gloss: A woman vachana poet of the movement, held by hagiographic tradition to be Siddharama's sister; her surviving vachanas add to the small but real body of women's voices in the sharana corpus alongside Akka Mahadevi's.
figures.facet: Her presence corrects a roster that would otherwise read as though Akka Mahadevi's radicalism were solitary — the movement's asserted gender egalitarianism produced more than one woman's name in the record, even if far fewer than the dozens attested in Kannada literary history.
figures.provenance: Vachana Sahitya; Virashaiva hagiographic tradition (family relation to Siddharama is hagiographic, not independently documented).

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: The absolute macrocosm anchored directly into the portable microcosm of the devotee.
Intra-tradition bond: The reformer and his primary weapon—the unmediated, vernacular word.
Intra-tradition bond: The two forms of the Linga—the personal static emblem on the body, and the living, moving embodiment in the mendicant.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Lingayat theology explicitly identifies the Upanishadic Brahman with Parasiva, the supreme, non-dual absolute.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The direct theological identification with the Vedic/Puranic Shiva, though completely stripped of his traditional mythology and temple-bound iconography.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An aniconic, dark stone serving as the absolute, physical focal point of divine presence, radically centralizing unmediated worship.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The fragment of the absolute carried directly upon or within the individual, rendering external churches and mediaries obsolete.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A physical, handheld object that serves as a direct, empowered locus of spiritual force, circumventing distant, unapproachable gods.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The rebel who bypasses the divine hierarchy and priesthood to bring the fire of liberation directly to the common humans.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ascetic reformer who explicitly rejects the authority of the Vedas, animal sacrifice, and the rigid stratification of the caste system.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The female figure who is stripped of all garments and societal regalia to enter the absolute truth of the divine realm.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The living human being who becomes the literal, moving embodiment and representative of the deity within the community.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The living human vehicle who actively channels and anchors the divine for the community, superseding static idols.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The mundane labor and physical structures of daily agricultural/craft life infused with the exact map of cosmic salvation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Finding the absolute and the divine not in ascetic withdrawal, but in the natural flow of daily, unforced physical action.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The power of the spoken, vernacular word acting as a divine force that gives literal shape to the cosmos and society.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The enigmatic master of esoteric wisdom who initiates true seekers into the absolute, hidden truth of the cosmos.
Intra-tradition bond: the founder and the nephew who systematized his doctrine and led the sharanas after Kalyana fell.
Intra-tradition bond: the systematizer and the six-stage doctrinal scheme his writings articulate.
Intra-tradition bond: the six-stage path culminates in aikya, the complete union of the anga (soul, borne on the ishtalinga) with the linga.
Intra-tradition bond: the reformer and the egalitarian assembly hall his movement established at Kalyana.
Intra-tradition bond: Allama presided over the hall from the Shunya Simhasana as its spiritual touchstone.
Intra-tradition bond: the founder and the eightfold daily discipline his movement instituted.
Intra-tradition bond: the founder and the hagiographic text that transmits his life and deeds.
Intra-tradition bond: Basava's chosen form of Shiva and signature ankita of nearly every vachana he composed.
Intra-tradition bond: the earliest major vachana poet and the vernacular verse form he demonstrates predates the Kalyana movement.
Intra-tradition bond: the tank-builder sharana and the 'work is heaven' doctrine his public works embody.
Intra-tradition bond: tradition folds his Sonnalige circle into the Anubhava Mantapa fellowship.
Intra-tradition bond: two women vachana poets of the same movement, distinct voices rather than duplicate figures.
Intra-tradition bond: hagiographic tradition holds her to be Siddharama's sister, though this family tie is not independently documented outside Virashaiva hagiography.
Both map the soul's approach to the divine as a fixed sequence of graded stages rather than a single undifferentiated leap.

The Lingayat ishtalinga descends directly from the Shaiva temple lingam's emblem but reverses its practice: the consecrated, stationary, priest-served temple axis becomes a personal object worn on the body from birth, and its theology explicitly rejects the mediated worship the Shaiva lingam requires.

dossier.essence: Lingayatism (Virashaivism) is a Shaiva devotional tradition and community that took form in 12th-century Karnataka around the poet-statesman Basava (Basavanna) and the assembly of sharanas at the Anubhava Mantapa. Lingayats worship Shiva as the formless absolute (Parashiva) through a personal ishtalinga worn on the body, and historically reject caste, temple and priestly mediation, ritual pollution, and rebirth.
dossier.cosmos: Parashiva is the formless absolute that genuinely modifies itself into world and soul, so the universe is real rather than illusion (maya) — a qualified nondualism (shakti-vishishtadvaita). The liberated soul unites with Shiva (aikya) at the end of a graded six-stage ascent (shatsthala) rather than transmigrating through further lives, which cuts against the mainstream karma-and-rebirth scheme. The divine is not housed in monumental temples but carried on each devotee's chest.
dossier.practice: The devotee constantly wears and worships the ishtalinga given at initiation; ethics turn on kayaka (one's labor as worship, 'work is heaven') and dasoha (sharing the fruits of that labor), and on reverence for the wandering ascetic (jangama) as the 'moving Shiva.' Lingayats are typically vegetarian and bury rather than cremate their dead, and the movement historically enacted radical social mixing across former castes.
dossier.canon: The vachanas — free-verse sayings in vernacular Kannada by Basava, Akka Mahadevi, Allama Prabhu, and other sharanas — with later systematizing texts such as the Shunyasampadane and the Siddhanta Shikhamani.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds portable, unmediated divinity — the absolute carried on the individual body, dissolving temple, priest, and caste, with ordinary work itself made the path to liberation.
sources: R. Blake Michael, 'The Origins of Virasaiva Sects' (1992)
J.P. Schouten, 'Revolution of the Mystics: On the Social Aspects of Virasaivism' (1995)
